messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Reward models for alignment require large preference datasets,
but high-quality human labels are scarce; how can we learn
reliable reward models from limited or noisy preferences?


### Proposed method
Build a bootstrapped, denoised preference pool before training the reward model. Start with AI-labeled preference pairs (or legacy human pairs if available) and re-query each pair multiple times using diverse labelers (e.g., several LLM judges or light-perturbed prompts) to obtain a consistency score c in [0,1]. Train a small ensemble of reward models on this pool and compute each pair’s average reward margin Δ̄ and ensemble disagreement σ_Δ. Prune contradictions (Δ̄ < 0 with high |Δ̄| and high c) and filter away trivially easy pairs (very large |Δ̄| with low σ_Δ), keeping a “core” set with moderate-to-large |Δ̄| but low σ_Δ, plus a frontier set of hard/ambiguous pairs (small |Δ̄| and/or high σ_Δ) for targeted review. If your preference data comes from multiple collection policies or labelers, estimate exposure propensities per context and source, and attach inverse-propensity weights to each pair to counter sampling bias before any filtering.

Triage scarce human effort to the frontier set with a monotone reward-difference view. Fit a provisional reward model on the core set and sort all pairs by its predicted reward differences. Detect elbow/knee points on the cumulative accuracy curve to split the distribution into easy/high-confidence, uncertain, and likely-mislabeled tails. Auto-accept the easy region after a tiny spot-check, flip labels only in the most extreme low-confidence tail flagged by the curve, and allocate human annotation starting from the elbow moving into the uncertain region. Prioritize pairs by a mixture score that combines low |Δ̄|, high σ_Δ, and low c (multi-query inconsistency), which concentrates annotation on items that both the ensemble and the provisional model find confusing.

Train the reward model with a confidence- and propensity-weighted pairwise objective that is robust to outliers. Use a Bradley–Terry/DPO-style loss where each pair’s contribution is weighted by: (i) the multi-query consistency c, (ii) the inverse propensity weight to debias selection, and (iii) an uncertainty-aware downweight w = 1 / (1 + σ_Δ). Replace the standard logistic with a heavy-tailed alternative (Student’s t link or an equivalent robust loss) so rare but large residuals are softly clipped instead of dominating updates. Add a small margin-shrinkage penalty that pulls very small predicted gaps toward zero, further neutralizing ambiguous pairs. Optionally learn a per-pair temperature (heteroscedastic head) that predicts noise scale and gates the loss, trained to match observed multi-query variability.

Close the loop with targeted data acquisition and safe downstream use. When collecting new model responses for comparison, rank multiple candidates per prompt with the current reward model and sample extremes (best and worst) much more often than mid-range items; this yields more informative preference updates and reduces exposure to the noisiest margin region. When using the reward model for RLHF, apply rank-based policy filtration to focus optimization on the reliably scored regions rather than mid-range plateaus, limiting over-optimization on spurious signals. Iterate: retrain the ensemble, refresh core/frontier sets, re-estimate propensities if sources shift, and spend incremental human budget only where the mixture score stays high.

### Rationale
This pipeline raises reward-model reliability under scarce or noisy preferences by combining three complementary controls. First, it reduces label noise upfront via multi-query consistency, ensemble disagreement filtering, and automatic detection of easy versus error-prone regions, so the model sees fewer contradictory and trivial pairs. Second, it maximizes the impact of limited human labels through principled triage on the reward-gap distribution, auditing only a tiny easy subset and concentrating annotation on ambiguous, disagreement-heavy pairs. Third, it trains with robustness and debiasing: confidence- and propensity-weighted losses, heavy-tailed error models, and margin regularization prevent outliers and selection effects from skewing the learned reward.

Finally, the same reliability lens guides both data acquisition and downstream RL: sampling extremes for comparisons increases information per label, and rank-based policy filtration during RL prevents the agent from exploiting mid-range noise. Together, these steps convert limited, noisy preferences into a denoised, debiased, and robust supervisory signal that transfers to more stable policy improvement.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [RL] Policy Filtration for RLHF to Mitigate Noise in Reward Models (2409.06957)
   problem: In RLHF for LLMs, reward models trained from human preferences are often inaccurate—especially in the mid-reward region—leading PPO to learn from noisy signals and over-optimize spurious reward.
   method:  Propose Policy Filtration for PPO (PF-PPO): for each prompt, sample N responses from the current policy, rank them with the reward model, and sample a training response according to a rank-based weight vector that emphasizes more reliable regions (e.g., Best-Random, Best-Worst) while de-emphasizing mid-range rewards. Train PPO to optimize a filtered policy µθ but, in practice, use samples collected from the unfiltered policy πθ as if from µθ, changing only the sampling stage. This increases the reward–score reliability (higher R^2) and improves downstream performance, achieving SOTA among 7B models on HumanEval/MBPP and large gains on the challenging LeetCode Contest.

2. [LLM_NLP] RLTHF: Targeted Human Feedback for LLM Alignment (2502.13417)
   problem: AI feedback (RLAIF) often mislabels hard preference pairs, while full human annotation is costly. We need a way to triage where humans should intervene and where LLM labels can be trusted to reach human-level alignment efficiently.
   method:  Train a reward model (RM) on the LLM-labeled preference data and compute reward differences Δ between chosen and rejected responses to form a monotonic reward distribution across the dataset. Detect the 'elbow' and 'knee' points via the first derivative to coarsely segment easy/high-accuracy vs. hard/low-accuracy regions. Treat upper-left (pre-elbow) samples as high-confidence AI-correct labels, and identify bottom-right samples as likely AI mislabels; flip labels for the most extreme mislabeled region and start human annotation at the reflection of the elbow (on the x-axis) moving leftward. Validate coarse agreement by sampling a tiny subset (<0.1%) from the easy region to guard against prompt/model misalignment.

3. [LLM_NLP] DPF-CM: A Data Processing Framework with Privacy-Preserving Vector Databases for Chinese Medical LLMs Training and Deployment (2509.01354)
   problem: Preference datasets for RLHF/DPO in the medical domain contain noisy pairs (contradictory, indistinguishable, or overly easy), which degrade training of reward models and downstream alignment.
   method:  Train multiple reward models (five with different seeds) and compute a per-sample preference distance rθ(x,yc)−rθ(x,yr); average distances across models to stabilize judgments. Filter out samples whose distances are consistently negative (label contradictions) or excessively large (trivial/easy) based on thresholds, thereby denoising the preference data. This ensemble-based filtering improves performance across multi-turn/single-turn dialogues, terminology, and benchmarks (Sec. 3.3; Table 12).

4. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

5. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

6. [MULTIMODAL] DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment (2304.04514)
   problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
   method:  DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).

7. [CV] Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (2107.11787)
   problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation trained with only image-level labels relies on sparse CAM seeds and off-the-shelf saliency maps that are used offline and not adapted to the target data, yielding low-quality pseudo labels and coarse boundaries.
   method:  AuxSegNet is a joint multi-task framework that treats semantic segmentation as the primary task and leverages multi-label image classification and saliency detection as auxiliary tasks, all sharing a backbone and trained end-to-end using only image-level labels and pre-trained saliency maps as initial pseudo labels. The classification branch produces CAMs; the saliency and segmentation branches use atrous conv decoders and are trained with losses on both initial and refined predictions against pseudo labels. Saliency pseudo labels are updated online by fusing the model’s refined saliency with pre-trained saliency through a dense CRF, while segmentation pseudo labels are generated from CAMs (and later their affinity-refined versions), enabling the auxiliary tasks to adapt to the target dataset and improve segmentation performance iteratively.

8. [MULTIMODAL] Gen3DEval: Using vLLMs for Automatic Evaluation of Generated 3D Objects (2504.08125)
   problem: There is a scarcity of diverse, labeled supervision that captures 3D-specific artifacts and nuanced text fidelity differences needed to train a reliable 3D quality evaluator.
   method:  The authors curate a composite dataset combining multi-view renders and captions of approximately 140k artist-created meshes, large-scale human pairwise preferences over outputs from numerous 3D generative methods, and extensive synthetic comparisons. Synthetic data is produced by perturbing meshes (e.g., Laplacian smoothing, bevels/extrusions, texture blur/seams), adding NeRF/Gaussian-splat artifacts (floaters, transparency, disconnected parts), and creating textual/appearance variants via Llama3.2 and multi-view video diffusion; low-relevance pairs are filtered using CLIP similarity. These VQA-style pairs supervise Gen3DEval to recognize appearance, surface, and text fidelity cues typical of modern 3D generators.

9. [RL] Navigating Noisy Feedback: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning with Error-Prone Language Models (2410.17389)
   problem: Lower-quality LLMs produce inconsistent rankings, degrading policy learning when relying on single noisy annotations; we need a simple way to make such feedback usable without human intervention.
   method:  Adopt a multi-query ranking strategy: query each state pair multiple times and compute confidence as the fraction of consistent preferences. Show that a confidence-weighted preference loss is equivalent to the standard loss over multi-query data (Lemma 1; Sec. 4.1; Appendix A), which naturally pushes score differences for inconsistent pairs toward zero. Synthetic studies demonstrate that with enough queries even datasets with ~60% ranking accuracy enable near-optimal learning (Fig. 7), and real experiments show 5-query potential-difference rewards with Mixtral outperform single-query and direct-reward baselines across tasks (Fig. 8; Sec. 5.4.2).

10. [RL] Deterministic and Discriminative Imitation (D2-Imitation): Revisiting Adversarial Imitation for Sample Efficiency (2112.06054)
   problem: Adversarial imitation learning is sample-inefficient and unstable: on-policy sampling is repeatedly required and off-policy extensions often change the objective or require challenging min-max optimization with non-stationary rewards.
   method:  Reformulate distribution matching via the stationarity equation into a successor-sample form and prove it preserves the same set of basic feasible solutions (deterministic policies) as the original predecessor-sample equation (Theorem 1). This enables a temporal-difference update (Eq. 6) to directly learn a proxy ˜d(s,a) of the expert state-action distribution from off-policy data, and to train a deterministic policy via deterministic policy gradients, avoiding adversarial training and density-ratio min-max optimization.

11. [ROBOTICS] MENTOR: Mixture-of-Experts Network with Task-Oriented Perturbation for Visual Reinforcement Learning (2410.14972)
   problem: Parameter perturbation helps visual RL agents escape local minima, but sampling perturbations from fixed random initializations ignores task structure, yielding inefficient exploration and unstable learning.
   method:  Introduce a task-oriented perturbation mechanism that maintains a fixed-size buffer S_top of top-performing historical agents. Fit a Gaussian Φ_oriented = N(μ_top^θ, σ_top^θ) over their weights and periodically sample a candidate ϕ from this distribution to perturb the current agent. Blend weights via θ ← αθ + (1−α)ϕ, where α is set by the dormant-ratio-based schedule α = clip(1 − μβ, α_min, α_max) (Sec. 3.2, Alg. 1; Sec. 2). This yields perturbations biased toward promising regions, leading to faster skill acquisition and consistently lower, smoother dormant ratios than random-noise perturbations (Fig. 5).

12. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

13. [IR_REC] Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation (1811.01802)
   problem: Unbiased learning-to-rank from clicks requires accurate examination-bias (propensity) estimates, but existing methods are restricted to the Position-Based Model (PBM) that ignores query/user context and often rely on intrusive randomized swaps or difficult document-level relevance modeling.
   method:  Introduce the Contextual Position-Based Model (CPBM), which models examination as Pr(E=1 | k, x) depending on rank k and context x, and learn it via a neural network h(k, x). Develop an AllPairs estimator that harvests implicit interventions from logs of at least two historic rankers (e.g., A/B tests or stationary deployments): define interventional sets S_{k,k'} of (x, d) that appear at both positions under different rankers, compute assignment weights q_k(x, d), form IPS-weighted click labels, and jointly fit h and an average-relevance model g(k, k', x) by maximizing a weighted cross-entropy objective. This controls for unobserved relevance without explicit swaps and yields context-specific propensities.

14. [RL] Navigating Noisy Feedback: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning with Error-Prone Language Models (2410.17389)
   problem: Lower-quality LLMs produce inconsistent rankings, degrading policy learning when relying on single noisy annotations; we need a simple way to make such feedback usable without human intervention.
   method:  Adopt a multi-query ranking strategy: query each state pair multiple times and compute confidence as the fraction of consistent preferences. Show that a confidence-weighted preference loss is equivalent to the standard loss over multi-query data (Lemma 1; Sec. 4.1; Appendix A), which naturally pushes score differences for inconsistent pairs toward zero. Synthetic studies demonstrate that with enough queries even datasets with ~60% ranking accuracy enable near-optimal learning (Fig. 7), and real experiments show 5-query potential-difference rewards with Mixtral outperform single-query and direct-reward baselines across tasks (Fig. 8; Sec. 5.4.2).

15. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

16. [SPEECH] Back Translation for Speech-to-text Translation Without Transcripts (2305.08709)
   problem: Synthetic speech-text pairs generated via back translation vary in quality; naively training on all can hurt ST performance, especially without transcripts.
   method:  Train a unit-to-target model to translate generated units back to target text and score each synthetic sample by BLEU(ŷ,y); keep only the top ρ fraction (ρ=75% performs best) and discard the rest. Use a 2-stage schedule: pre-train the ST model on the selected synthetic data, then fine-tune on real ST data to mitigate noise. This filtering and training strategy improves MuST-C En→De from 26.2 to 26.6 BLEU at ρ=75% and underpins the overall +2.3 BLEU gains.

17. [SPEECH] IMPACT: Iterative Mask-based Parallel Decoding for Text-to-Audio Generation with Diffusion Modeling (2506.00736)
   problem: Text-conditional training must simultaneously learn audio generation and text alignment, but paired text–audio data is limited, reducing fidelity and text relevance.
   method:  IMPACT introduces an unconditional pre-training phase (mask-based generative modeling without text) on large unpaired audio (e.g., AudioSet), followed by text-conditional training. This curriculum first imparts general audio generative ability, then aligns to text, enabling the use of much larger datasets. Ablations show unconditional pre-training is indispensable: models with pre-training consistently improve FD/FAD and human REL/OVL ratings, with larger-scale pre-training further boosting fidelity despite minor trade-offs on some metrics.

18. [SPEECH] UniCATS: A Unified Context-Aware Text-to-Speech Framework with Contextual VQ-Diffusion and Vocoding (2306.07547)
   problem: Vocoding from semantic tokens lacks speaker identity, and prior approaches rely on speaker embeddings (e.g., x-vectors) or acoustic tokens from audio codecs, which can bottleneck quality or require codec dependencies.
   method:  CTX-vec2wav is a contextual vocoder that converts semantic tokens to waveforms while conditioning on acoustic context provided directly by mel-spectrograms from surrounding audio. It uses two Conformer-based semantic encoders augmented with cross-attention to mel features produced by a lightweight mel encoder (1D conv), eliminating the need for speaker embeddings or codec acoustic tokens. An auxiliary feature adaptor conditions on and predicts pitch, energy, and POV to improve fidelity. Training splits each utterance: a 2–3 s left segment supplies mel context, and the remaining segment supplies semantic tokens for vocoding.

19. [ROBOTICS] Gait in Eight: Efficient On-Robot Learning for Omnidirectional Quadruped Locomotion (2503.08375)
   problem: Extending on-robot learning from fixed forward motion to omnidirectional locomotion is difficult, especially with only proprioceptive sensing and noisy, biased trunk velocity estimates (no foot contact sensors).
   method:  Define an omnidirectional velocity-tracking task and reward using only proprioceptive signals. Estimate trunk orientation via magnetometer/accelerometer fusion and linear velocity via a Kalman filter fusing IMU accelerations with leg odometry. Combine this with a CPG-based controller that provides a stable in-place trot; the policy outputs per-foot Cartesian offsets and receives the gait phase, enabling stable learning and successful outdoor omnidirectional gaits (≈0.25 m/s in x, 0.15 m/s in y).

The final plan is the time-wise mean of the fitted t-distribution, effectively ignoring hallucinated trajectories. Ablations show ν=1.5 outperforms Gaussian (ν→∞), and RIP improves success rates by 26% over KAT and 16% over a Gaussian aggregator across 10 manipulation tasks, especially in low-data regimes.

Finally, we employ dual-weighted trajectory aggregation to synthesize a robust consensus from multiple reasoning paths. 1. Omni-RRM: Advancing Omni Reward Modeling via Automatic Rubric-Grounded Preference Synthesis (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 3)

A critical bottleneck remains the lack of effective reward models (RMs): existing RMs are predominantly vision-centric, return opaque scalar scores, and rely on costly human annotations. We introduce \textbf{Omni-RRM}, the first open-source rubric-grounded reward model that produces structured, multi-dimension preference judgments with dimension-wise justifications across \textbf{text, image, video, and audio}. At the core of our approach is \textbf{Omni-Preference}, a large-scale dataset built via a fully automated pipeline: we synthesize candidate response pairs by contrasting models of different capabilities, and use strong teacher models to \emph{reconcile and filter} preferences while providing a modality-aware \emph{rubric-grounded rationale} for each pair. This eliminates the need for human-labeled training preferences. Omni-RRM is trained in two stages: supervised fine-tuning to learn the rubric-grounded outputs, followed by reinforcement learning (GRPO) to sharpen discrimination on difficult, low-contrast pairs. Comprehensive evaluations show that Omni-RRM achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on video (80.2\% on ShareGPT-V) and audio (66.8\% on Audio-HH-RLHF) benchmarks, and substantially outperforms existing open-source RMs on image tasks, with a 17.7\% absolute gain over its base model on overall accuracy. Omni-RRM also improves downstream performance via Best-of-$N$ selection and transfers to text-only preference benchmarks. Our data, code, and models are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Omni-RRM-CC08.

2. Uncertainty-Aware Reward Discounting for Mitigating Reward Hacking ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Reinforcement learning (RL) systems typically optimize scalar reward functions that assume precise and reliable evaluation of outcomes. However, real-world objectives--especially those derived from human preferences--are often uncertain, context-dependent, and internally inconsistent. This mismatch can lead to alignment failures such as reward hacking, over-optimization, and overconfident behavior. We introduce a dual-source uncertainty-aware reward framework that explicitly models both epistemic uncertainty in value estimation and uncertainty in human preferences. Model uncertainty is captured via ensemble disagreement over value predictions, while preference uncertainty is derived from variability in reward annotations. We combine these signals through a confidence-adjusted Reliability Filter that adaptively modulates action selection, encouraging a balance between exploitation and caution. Empirical results across multiple discrete grid configurations (6x6, 8x8, 10x10) and high-dimensional continuous control environments (Hopper-v4, Walker2d-v4) demonstrate that our approach yields more stable training dynamics and reduces exploitative behaviors under reward ambiguity, achieving a 93.7% reduction in reward-hacking behavior as measured by trap visitation frequency. We demonstrate statistical significance of these improvements and robustness under up to 30% supervisory noise, albeit with a trade-off in peak observed reward compared to unconstrained baselines. By treating uncertainty as a first-class component of the reward signal, this work offers a principled approach toward more reliable and aligned reinforcement learning systems.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Reward models for alignment require large preference datasets,
but high-quality human labels are scarce; how can we learn
reliable reward models from limited or noisy preferences?


### Proposed method
Build a bootstrapped, denoised preference pool before training the reward model. Start with AI-labeled preference pairs (or legacy human pairs if available) and re-query each pair multiple times using diverse labelers (e.g., several LLM judges or light-perturbed prompts) to obtain a consistency score c in [0,1]. Train a small ensemble of reward models on this pool and compute each pair’s average reward margin Δ̄ and ensemble disagreement σ_Δ. Prune contradictions (Δ̄ < 0 with high |Δ̄| and high c) and filter away trivially easy pairs (very large |Δ̄| with low σ_Δ), keeping a “core” set with moderate-to-large |Δ̄| but low σ_Δ, plus a frontier set of hard/ambiguous pairs (small |Δ̄| and/or high σ_Δ) for targeted review. If your preference data comes from multiple collection policies or labelers, estimate exposure propensities per context and source, and attach inverse-propensity weights to each pair to counter sampling bias before any filtering.

Triage scarce human effort to the frontier set with a monotone reward-difference view. Fit a provisional reward model on the core set and sort all pairs by its predicted reward differences. Detect elbow/knee points on the cumulative accuracy curve to split the distribution into easy/high-confidence, uncertain, and likely-mislabeled tails. Auto-accept the easy region after a tiny spot-check, flip labels only in the most extreme low-confidence tail flagged by the curve, and allocate human annotation starting from the elbow moving into the uncertain region. Prioritize pairs by a mixture score that combines low |Δ̄|, high σ_Δ, and low c (multi-query inconsistency), which concentrates annotation on items that both the ensemble and the provisional model find confusing.

Train the reward model with a confidence- and propensity-weighted pairwise objective that is robust to outliers. Use a Bradley–Terry/DPO-style loss where each pair’s contribution is weighted by: (i) the multi-query consistency c, (ii) the inverse propensity weight to debias selection, and (iii) an uncertainty-aware downweight w = 1 / (1 + σ_Δ). Replace the standard logistic with a heavy-tailed alternative (Student’s t link or an equivalent robust loss) so rare but large residuals are softly clipped instead of dominating updates. Add a small margin-shrinkage penalty that pulls very small predicted gaps toward zero, further neutralizing ambiguous pairs. Optionally learn a per-pair temperature (heteroscedastic head) that predicts noise scale and gates the loss, trained to match observed multi-query variability.

Close the loop with targeted data acquisition and safe downstream use. When collecting new model responses for comparison, rank multiple candidates per prompt with the current reward model and sample extremes (best and worst) much more often than mid-range items; this yields more informative preference updates and reduces exposure to the noisiest margin region. When using the reward model for RLHF, apply rank-based policy filtration to focus optimization on the reliably scored regions rather than mid-range plateaus, limiting over-optimization on spurious signals. Iterate: retrain the ensemble, refresh core/frontier sets, re-estimate propensities if sources shift, and spend incremental human budget only where the mixture score stays high.

### Rationale
This pipeline raises reward-model reliability under scarce or noisy preferences by combining three complementary controls. First, it reduces label noise upfront via multi-query consistency, ensemble disagreement filtering, and automatic detection of easy versus error-prone regions, so the model sees fewer contradictory and trivial pairs. Second, it maximizes the impact of limited human labels through principled triage on the reward-gap distribution, auditing only a tiny easy subset and concentrating annotation on ambiguous, disagreement-heavy pairs. Third, it trains with robustness and debiasing: confidence- and propensity-weighted losses, heavy-tailed error models, and margin regularization prevent outliers and selection effects from skewing the learned reward.

Finally, the same reliability lens guides both data acquisition and downstream RL: sampling extremes for comparisons increases information per label, and rank-based policy filtration during RL prevents the agent from exploiting mid-range noise. Together, these steps convert limited, noisy preferences into a denoised, debiased, and robust supervisory signal that transfers to more stable policy improvement.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [RL] Policy Filtration for RLHF to Mitigate Noise in Reward Models (2409.06957)
   problem: In RLHF for LLMs, reward models trained from human preferences are often inaccurate—especially in the mid-reward region—leading PPO to learn from noisy signals and over-optimize spurious reward.
   method:  Propose Policy Filtration for PPO (PF-PPO): for each prompt, sample N responses from the current policy, rank them with the reward model, and sample a training response according to a rank-based weight vector that emphasizes more reliable regions (e.g., Best-Random, Best-Worst) while de-emphasizing mid-range rewards. Train PPO to optimize a filtered policy µθ but, in practice, use samples collected from the unfiltered policy πθ as if from µθ, changing only the sampling stage. This increases the reward–score reliability (higher R^2) and improves downstream performance, achieving SOTA among 7B models on HumanEval/MBPP and large gains on the challenging LeetCode Contest.

2. [LLM_NLP] RLTHF: Targeted Human Feedback for LLM Alignment (2502.13417)
   problem: AI feedback (RLAIF) often mislabels hard preference pairs, while full human annotation is costly. We need a way to triage where humans should intervene and where LLM labels can be trusted to reach human-level alignment efficiently.
   method:  Train a reward model (RM) on the LLM-labeled preference data and compute reward differences Δ between chosen and rejected responses to form a monotonic reward distribution across the dataset. Detect the 'elbow' and 'knee' points via the first derivative to coarsely segment easy/high-accuracy vs. hard/low-accuracy regions. Treat upper-left (pre-elbow) samples as high-confidence AI-correct labels, and identify bottom-right samples as likely AI mislabels; flip labels for the most extreme mislabeled region and start human annotation at the reflection of the elbow (on the x-axis) moving leftward. Validate coarse agreement by sampling a tiny subset (<0.1%) from the easy region to guard against prompt/model misalignment.

3. [LLM_NLP] DPF-CM: A Data Processing Framework with Privacy-Preserving Vector Databases for Chinese Medical LLMs Training and Deployment (2509.01354)
   problem: Preference datasets for RLHF/DPO in the medical domain contain noisy pairs (contradictory, indistinguishable, or overly easy), which degrade training of reward models and downstream alignment.
   method:  Train multiple reward models (five with different seeds) and compute a per-sample preference distance rθ(x,yc)−rθ(x,yr); average distances across models to stabilize judgments. Filter out samples whose distances are consistently negative (label contradictions) or excessively large (trivial/easy) based on thresholds, thereby denoising the preference data. This ensemble-based filtering improves performance across multi-turn/single-turn dialogues, terminology, and benchmarks (Sec. 3.3; Table 12).

4. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

5. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)

Blend weights via θ ← αθ + (1−α)ϕ, where α is set by the dormant-ratio-based schedule α = clip(1 − μβ, α_min, α_max) (Sec. 3.2, Alg. 1; Sec. 2). 1. Multi-Stage Confidence-Guided Diffusion and Emotional Bidirectional Mamba for Robust Speech Emotion Recognition (IEEE Signal Processing Letters 2025, cites: 2)
   Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) in noisy environments is challenging due to the overlap between emotional and noise-related signals. We propose a novel emotion-diffusion approach to enhance SER performance in noisy conditions that transfers emotional information from clean to noisy speech through a multi-stage diffusion process. First, Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients and their delta features are extracted to capture emotional dynamics. Additionally, an Emotional Bidirectional Mamba Encoder with a Multi-time View Bidirectional State Space Model is designed to capture temporal emotional patterns. Next, the Emotion-Transferring Diffusion Network (ETDN) applies confidence filtering to retain key emotional features, ensuring effective emotion transfer despite noise. Finally, the Confidence-Guided Mutual Learning strategy refines noisy features for the Classification Network (CN), while the CN supervises the ETDN to maintain label consistency. Experiments on the IEMOCAP dataset show significant improvements in weighted accuracy, unweighted accuracy, and weighted F1 score across various signal-to-noise ratios.

2. Privacy-Aware ML Framework for Dynamic Query Formation in Multi-Dimensional Data (International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications 2025, cites: 0)
   — Interactive data exploration at scale remains constrained by 1) weak adaptability to shifting query workloads, 2) limited and post hoc error guarantees, 3) poor scalability under dynamic, high-dimensional data, 4) sparse user guidance during query formulation, and 5) non-trivial system overheads from learned or probabilistic components. We propose an end-to-end, privacy-aware framework that dynamically forms SQL queries for multi-dimensional data using randomized signals derived from personal web usage. The method integrates: 1) on-device user modeling that converts browsing interactions into preference embeddings under local differential privacy; 2) a constrained-randomization layer that enforces coverage and diversity to avoid filter bubbles while remaining responsive to user intent; 3) a contextual bandit policy (with optional deep reinforcement learning extension) that selects or completes query templates using signals from user profiles, session context, and data synopses; and 4) an error-aware AQP executor combining stratified/pilot sampling, synopsis reuse, and confidence-interval gating with automatic sample escalation. This design directly addresses the above limitations: the bandit adapts online to workload shifts; the AQP layer provides pre-execution feasibility checks and per-query error control; synopsis reuse and AB-tree – style random sampling maintain low latency under updates; and a guidance module (predictive autocompletion with information-gain scoring) reduces user effort while preserving exploration diversity. To evaluate effectiveness, we introduce a privacy-preserving training regimen (federated updates over DP-noised profiles) and a novel benchmark protocol measuring time-to-insight, error compliance under differential privacy, session diversity, and latency against strong baselines. The result is an ML-driven exploration loop that achieves error-bounded interactivity, robust personalization, and scalable performance on evolving, high-dimensional datasets, while providing evaluation metrics that capture both user experience and privacy-preserving guarantees.

3. Memory-T1: Reinforcement Learning for Temporal Reasoning in Multi-session Agents (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Temporal reasoning over long, multi-session dialogues is a critical capability for conversational agents. However, existing works and our pilot study have shown that as dialogue histories grow in length and accumulate noise, current long-context models struggle to accurately identify temporally pertinent information, significantly impairing reasoning performance. To address this, we introduce Memory-T1, a framework that learns a time-aware memory selection policy using reinforcement learning (RL). It employs a coarse-to-fine strategy, first pruning the dialogue history into a candidate set using temporal and relevance filters, followed by an RL agent that selects the precise evidence sessions. The RL training is guided by a multi-level reward function optimizing (i) answer accuracy, (ii) evidence grounding, and (iii) temporal consistency. In particular, the temporal consistency reward provides a dense signal by evaluating alignment with the query time scope at both the session-level (chronological proximity) and the utterance-level (chronological fidelity), enabling the agent to resolve subtle chronological ambiguities. On the Time-Dialog benchmark, Memory-T1 boosts a 7B model to an overall score of 67.0\%, establishing a new state-of-the-art performance for open-source models and outperforming a 14B baseline by 10.2\%. Ablation studies show temporal consistency and evidence grounding rewards jointly contribute to a 15.0\% performance gain. Moreover, Memory-T1 maintains robustness up to 128k tokens, where baseline models collapse, proving effectiveness against noise in extensive dialogue histories.    Federated learning enables privacy-preserving model training across distributed clients, yet real-world deployments face statistical, system, and behavioral heterogeneity, which degrades performance and increases vulnerability to adversarial clients. 7. JudgeSQL: Reasoning over SQL Candidates with Weighted Consensus Tournament (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Text-to-SQL is a pivotal task that bridges natural language understanding and structured data access, yet it remains fundamentally challenging due to semantic ambiguity and complex compositional reasoning. While large language models (LLMs) have greatly advanced SQL generation though prompting, supervised finetuning and reinforced tuning, the shift toward test-time scaling exposes a new bottleneck: selecting the correct query from a diverse candidate pool. Existing selection approaches, such as self-consistency or best-of-$N$ decoding, provide only shallow signals, making them prone to inconsistent scoring, fragile reasoning chains, and a failure to capture fine-grained semantic distinctions between closely related SQL candidates. Building on this, a weighted consensus tournament integrates explicit reasoning preferences with implicit generator confidence, yielding selections that are both more reliable and more efficient. Extensive experiments on the BIRD benchmark demonstrate that JudgeSQL exhibits superior SQL judgment capabilities and good cross-scale generalization and robustness to generator capacity.

TableMind internalizes planning, action, and reflection through a two-stage training strategy involving supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on filtered high-quality data and reinforcement learning (RL) via a multi-perspective reward and the Rank-Aware Policy Optimization (RAPO) algorithm. While TableMind establishes a solid foundation for programmatic agents, the inherent stochasticity of LLMs remains a critical challenge that leads to hallucinations. To ensure execution precision, we introduce confidence-based action refinement which monitors token-level probabilities to detect and self-correct syntactic noise for aleatoric uncertainty mitigation. Finally, we employ dual-weighted trajectory aggregation to synthesize a robust consensus from multiple reasoning paths. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that TableMind++ consistently outperforms previous baselines and proprietary models to validate the effectiveness of integrating autonomous training with uncertainty quantification. 1. Omni-RRM: Advancing Omni Reward Modeling via Automatic Rubric-Grounded Preference Synthesis (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 3)
   Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities, yet their performance is often capped by the coarse nature of existing alignment techniques. A critical bottleneck remains the lack of effective reward models (RMs): existing RMs are predominantly vision-centric, return opaque scalar scores, and rely on costly human annotations. We introduce \textbf{Omni-RRM}, the first open-source rubric-grounded reward model that produces structured, multi-dimension preference judgments with dimension-wise justifications across \textbf{text, image, video, and audio}. At the core of our approach is \textbf{Omni-Preference}, a large-scale dataset built via a fully automated pipeline: we synthesize candidate response pairs by contrasting models of different capabilities, and use strong teacher models to \emph{reconcile and filter} preferences while providing a modality-aware \emph{rubric-grounded rationale} for each pair. This eliminates the need for human-labeled training preferences. Omni-RRM is trained in two stages: supervised fine-tuning to learn the rubric-grounded outputs, followed by reinforcement learning (GRPO) to sharpen discrimination on difficult, low-contrast pairs. Comprehensive evaluations show that Omni-RRM achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on video (80.2\% on ShareGPT-V) and audio (66.8\% on Audio-HH-RLHF) benchmarks, and substantially outperforms existing open-source RMs on image tasks, with a 17.7\% absolute gain over its base model on overall accuracy. Omni-RRM also improves downstream performance via Best-of-$N$ selection and transfers to text-only preference benchmarks. Our data, code, and models are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Omni-RRM-CC08.

2. Uncertainty-Aware Reward Discounting for Mitigating Reward Hacking ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Reinforcement learning (RL) systems typically optimize scalar reward functions that assume precise and reliable evaluation of outcomes. However, real-world objectives--especially those derived from human preferences--are often uncertain, context-dependent, and internally inconsistent. This mismatch can lead to alignment failures such as reward hacking, over-optimization, and overconfident behavior. We introduce a dual-source uncertainty-aware reward framework that explicitly models both epistemic uncertainty in value estimation and uncertainty in human preferences. Model uncertainty is captured via ensemble disagreement over value predictions, while preference uncertainty is derived from variability in reward annotations. We combine these signals through a confidence-adjusted Reliability Filter that adaptively modulates action selection, encouraging a balance between exploitation and caution. Empirical results across multiple discrete grid configurations (6x6, 8x8, 10x10) and high-dimensional continuous control environments (Hopper-v4, Walker2d-v4) demonstrate that our approach yields more stable training dynamics and reduces exploitative behaviors under reward ambiguity, achieving a 93.7% reduction in reward-hacking behavior as measured by trap visitation frequency. We demonstrate statistical significance of these improvements and robustness under up to 30% supervisory noise, albeit with a trade-off in peak observed reward compared to unconstrained baselines. By treating uncertainty as a first-class component of the reward signal, this work offers a principled approach toward more reliable and aligned reinforcement learning systems.

   Traditional RLHF-based LLM alignment methods explicitly maximize the expected rewards from a separate reward model. More recent supervised alignment methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) circumvent this phase to avoid problems including model drift and reward overfitting. Although popular due to its simplicity, DPO and similar direct alignment methods which rely heavily on the Bradley-Terry-based pairwise preference formulation can still lead to degenerate policies when challenged by non-deterministic or noisy preference labels, for example human scoring of two candidate outputs with low confidence. This paper introduces DRDO (Direct Reward Distillation and policy-Optimization), which simultaneously models rewards and preferences to avoid such degeneracy. DRDO directly mimics rewards assigned by an oracle while learning human preferences with a novel preference likelihood formulation. Results on the Ultrafeedback and TL;DR datasets demonstrate that DRDO-trained policies surpass methods such as DPO and e-DPO in terms of expected rewards and are more robust, on average, to noisy preference signals as well as out-of-distribution (OOD) settings.
