messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Reward models for alignment require large preference datasets,
but high-quality human labels are scarce; how can we learn
reliable reward models from limited or noisy preferences?


### Proposed method
We propose Tri-Source Uncertainty-Calibrated Reward Modeling (TRU-RM), a training loop that learns a reliable reward model rθ with three supervision streams: scarce human pairwise preferences (gold), plentiful pseudo-preferences from a pseudo-interactive demonstrator (weak), and pointwise outcome proxies from offline logs (implicit). The reward model has a shared trunk and per-source calibration heads that learn small, source-specific logit offsets to capture systematic bias. We insert a discriminative prototype layer in the pre-logit space and attach an uncertainty head uϕ that outputs a per-comparison confidence used to weight, filter, and schedule updates. A lightweight hierarchical prior encourages small source-specific biases while preserving a strong shared core, and we update the structured prior with a sampling-based gradient estimator to avoid expensive full Bayesian inference.

To expand supervision beyond scarce human labels, we train a pseudo-interactive demonstrator (PID) that, given a task instance, proposes multiple candidate outputs/trajectories and selects pseudo-winners based on accessible structure: distance-to-goal or constraint satisfaction in RL-style tasks, or rubric-based automatic judges (readability, factuality, instruction adherence) for text. The PID generates pairwise pseudo-labels across diverse difficulty levels; these are only accepted when the reward model’s uncertainty uϕ is low and the PID’s margin is high. Outcome proxies (success/penalty rates, rule violations, hallucination flags) supply pointwise targets that the reward head also regressively matches, tying the pairwise objective to measurable signals.

Training interleaves three losses with uncertainty-aware weighting: (1) a Bradley–Terry (or DPO-style) pairwise loss on human comparisons (unit weight), (2) the same loss on PID comparisons down-weighted by per-example confidence and a per-source uncertainty EMA, and (3) a pointwise regression/classification loss on outcome proxies with temperature-scaled targets. We maintain per-source moving averages of uncertainty (U-EMA) to adapt global weights online: noisier sources are down-weighted until they become reliable. Source-specific calibration heads are L2-regularized toward zero on batches that include any human-anchored examples, keeping weak-supervision biases from contaminating the shared trunk.

To spend human labels where they matter most, TRU-RM performs uncertainty-aware, efficient elicitation. For each task instance, we sample k diverse candidates, rank them with the pointwise reward, and run a small number of right-to-left adjacent comparisons among the top-k to identify only the most informative pairs to ask humans, prioritizing adjacent pairs with high disagreement or variance from uϕ. This yields near listwise refinement at O(k) human queries per instance. Periodically, we also query “anchor” comparisons drawn from a static reference set to recalibrate and audit the model.

Finally, to prevent self-reward bias and drift, we include a debiasing monitor: a held-out suite scored by external references (task-specific metrics or curated gold comparisons). We regularize the reward model to maintain correlation with these references and limit candidate pool growth in self-improvement phases that would otherwise amplify stylistic or fluency artifacts. Together, the PID, uncertainty-weighted multi-source training, efficient elicitation, and debiasing yield a robust reward model from limited and noisy preference data.

### Rationale
TRU-RM attacks scarcity by converting abundant structure into supervision: a pseudo-demonstrator generates many high-margin comparisons, and offline outcomes supply pointwise targets. It controls noise via single-pass latent uncertainty to filter and weight pseudo-labels, plus per-source U-EMA that automatically down-weights unreliable streams. Active query selection concentrates human effort on the smallest set of pairs that maximally reduce uncertainty, using efficient adjacent comparisons to approach listwise benefits without quadratic query costs.

A shared-core, source-calibrated design lets the model learn a general reward signal while explicitly modeling biases from weak supervision, and a sampling-based structured prior improves data efficiency without heavy inference. Debiasing anchored to external references prevents the classic failure mode of self-reward pipelines that overvalue fluency or style. The result is a reward model that is better calibrated, more sample-efficient, and robust to noisy or synthetic preferences.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

8. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

11. [MULTIMODAL] Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning (2412.02071)
   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. The community has begun establishing best practices for evaluating reward models, from the development of benchmarks that test capabilities in specific skill areas to others that test agreement with human preferences. Compared to most other benchmarks, RewardBench 2 sources new human prompts instead of existing prompts from downstream evaluations, facilitating more rigorous evaluation practices. HADA wraps any algorithm or LLM in role-specific stakeholder agents -- business, data-science, audit, ethics, and customer -- each exposing conversational APIs so that technical and non-technical actors can query, steer, audit, or contest every decision across strategic, tactical, and real-time horizons. Alignment objectives, KPIs, and value constraints are expressed in natural language and are continuously propagated, logged, and versioned while thousands of heterogeneous agents run on different orchestration stacks. Walkthrough observation and log inspection demonstrated complete coverage of six predefined objectives: every role could invoke conversational control, trace KPIs and value constraints, detect and mitigate ZIP-code bias, and reproduce full decision lineage, independent of the underlying LLM or agent library. Contributions: (1) an open-source HADA architecture, (2) a mid-range design theory for human-AI alignment in multi-agent systems, and (3) empirical evidence that framework-agnostic, protocol-compliant stakeholder agents improve accuracy, transparency, and ethical compliance in real-world decision pipelines.

While numerous publicly available preference datasets provide pairwise comparisons of responses, the potential for biases in the resulting reward models remains underexplored. In this work, we introduce novel methods to detect and evaluate prefix bias—a systematic shift in model preferences triggered by minor variations in query prefixes—in LLM-based reward models trained on such datasets. We leverage these metrics to reveal significant biases in preference models across racial and gender dimensions. Our comprehensive evaluation spans diverse open-source preference datasets and reward model architectures, demonstrating susceptibility to this kind of bias regardless of the underlying model architecture. Furthermore, we propose a data augmentation strategy to mitigate these biases, showing its effectiveness in reducing the impact of prefix bias. We show that the selected hybrid mixture of synthetic and direct human preferences using HyPER achieves better RM performance compared to using either one exclusively by 7-13% on RewardBench and generalizes across unseen preference datasets and other base models. Finally, we analyze features from HyPER and find that prompts with moderate safety concerns or complexity benefit the most from human feedback.

However, it remains unclear how to evaluate and select a reliable RM for preference alignment in RALMs. To this end, we propose RAG-RewardBench, the first benchmark for evaluating RMs in RAG settings. First, we design four crucial and challenging RAG-specific scenarios to assess RMs, including multi-hop reasoning, fine-grained citation, appropriate abstain, and conflict robustness. Then, we incorporate 18 RAG subsets, six retrievers, and 24 RALMs to increase the diversity of data sources. Finally, we adopt an LLM-as-a-judge approach to improve preference annotation efficiency and effectiveness, exhibiting a strong correlation with human annotations. Based on the RAG-RewardBench, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of 45 RMs and uncover their limitations in RAG scenarios. Additionally, we also reveal that existing trained RALMs show almost no improvement in preference alignment, highlighting the need for a shift towards preference-aligned training.We release our benchmark and code publicly at https://huggingface.co/datasets/jinzhuoran/RAG-RewardBench/ for future work.

6. AlignSurvey: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Human Preferences Alignment in Social Surveys (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2025, cites: 1)
   Understanding human attitudes, preferences, and behaviors through social surveys is essential for academic research and policymaking. While recent studies explore large language models (LLMs) to simulate survey responses, most are limited to structured questions, overlook the entire survey process, and risks under-representing marginalized groups due to training data biases. We introduce AlignSurvey, the first benchmark that systematically replicates and evaluates the full social survey pipeline using LLMs. It defines four tasks aligned with key survey stages: social role modeling, semi-structured interview modeling, attitude stance modeling and survey response modeling. It also provides task-specific evaluation metrics to assess alignment fidelity, consistency, and fairness at both individual and group levels, with a focus on demographic diversity. To support AlignSurvey, we construct a multi-tiered dataset architecture: (i) the Social Foundation Corpus, a cross-national resource with 44K+ interview dialogues and 400K+ structured survey records; and (ii) a suite of Entire-Pipeline Survey Datasets, including the expert-annotated AlignSurvey-Expert (ASE) and two nationally representative surveys for cross-cultural evaluation.    Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a transformative methodology in the development of conversational artificial intelligence systems. Unlike traditional supervised learning approaches, RLHF optimizes for subjective human preferences rather than objective metrics, resulting in AI systems that better align with human values and expectations. The implementation follows a multi-stage process including supervised fine-tuning, reward model training, and reinforcement learning optimization. While highly effective at improving model helpfulness, reducing harmful outputs, and enhancing factual consistency, RLHF implementation presents significant challenges related to data quality, scalability, reward hacking, and distribution shift. Ethical considerations surrounding bias, transparency, power dynamics, and long-term value alignment further complicate responsible deployment. Various strategies can address these challenges, including diverse annotator selection, constitutional principles, hybrid evaluation systems, and robust transparency measures. Looking forward, emerging trends such as self-supervised preference learning, multi-objective optimization, user-specific adaptation, and computational efficiency improvements will likely shape the continued evolution of this field as conversational AI becomes increasingly integrated across healthcare, customer service, education, and enterprise applications.

9. A Statistical Framework for Alignment with Biased AI Feedback (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 2)
   Modern alignment pipelines are increasingly replacing expensive human preference labels with evaluations from large language models (LLM-as-Judge). However, AI labels can be systematically biased compared to high-quality human feedback datasets. In this paper, we develop two debiased alignment methods within a general framework that accommodates heterogeneous prompt-response distributions and external human feedback sources. Debiased Direct Preference Optimization (DDPO) augments standard DPO with a residual-based correction and density-ratio reweighting to mitigate systematic bias, while retaining DPO's computational efficiency. Debiased Identity Preference Optimization (DIPO) directly estimates human preference probabilities without imposing a parametric reward model. We provide theoretical guarantees for both methods: DDPO offers a practical and computationally efficient solution for large-scale alignment, whereas DIPO serves as a robust, statistically optimal alternative that attains the semiparametric efficiency bound. Empirical studies on sentiment generation, summarization, and single-turn dialogue demonstrate that the proposed methods substantially improve alignment efficiency and recover performance close to that of an oracle trained on fully human-labeled data.

In this work, we confront the reward overoptimization problem in diffusion model alignment through the lenses of both inductive and primacy biases. We first identify a mismatch between current methods and the temporal inductive bias inherent in the multi-step denoising process of diffusion models, as a potential source of reward overoptimization. Then, we surprisingly discover that dormant neurons in our critic model act as a regularization against reward overoptimization while active neurons reflect primacy bias. Motivated by these observations, we propose Temporal Diffusion Policy Optimization with critic active neuron Reset (TDPO-R), a policy gradient algorithm that exploits the temporal inductive bias of diffusion models and mitigates the primacy bias stemming from active neurons. Empirical results demonstrate the superior efficacy of our methods in mitigating reward overoptimization. Code is avaliable at https://github.com/ZiyiZhang27/tdpo.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Reward models for alignment require large preference datasets,
but high-quality human labels are scarce; how can we learn
reliable reward models from limited or noisy preferences?


### Proposed method
We propose Tri-Source Uncertainty-Calibrated Reward Modeling (TRU-RM), a training loop that learns a reliable reward model rθ with three supervision streams: scarce human pairwise preferences (gold), plentiful pseudo-preferences from a pseudo-interactive demonstrator (weak), and pointwise outcome proxies from offline logs (implicit). The reward model has a shared trunk and per-source calibration heads that learn small, source-specific logit offsets to capture systematic bias. We insert a discriminative prototype layer in the pre-logit space and attach an uncertainty head uϕ that outputs a per-comparison confidence used to weight, filter, and schedule updates. A lightweight hierarchical prior encourages small source-specific biases while preserving a strong shared core, and we update the structured prior with a sampling-based gradient estimator to avoid expensive full Bayesian inference.

To expand supervision beyond scarce human labels, we train a pseudo-interactive demonstrator (PID) that, given a task instance, proposes multiple candidate outputs/trajectories and selects pseudo-winners based on accessible structure: distance-to-goal or constraint satisfaction in RL-style tasks, or rubric-based automatic judges (readability, factuality, instruction adherence) for text. The PID generates pairwise pseudo-labels across diverse difficulty levels; these are only accepted when the reward model’s uncertainty uϕ is low and the PID’s margin is high. Outcome proxies (success/penalty rates, rule violations, hallucination flags) supply pointwise targets that the reward head also regressively matches, tying the pairwise objective to measurable signals.

Training interleaves three losses with uncertainty-aware weighting: (1) a Bradley–Terry (or DPO-style) pairwise loss on human comparisons (unit weight), (2) the same loss on PID comparisons down-weighted by per-example confidence and a per-source uncertainty EMA, and (3) a pointwise regression/classification loss on outcome proxies with temperature-scaled targets. We maintain per-source moving averages of uncertainty (U-EMA) to adapt global weights online: noisier sources are down-weighted until they become reliable. Source-specific calibration heads are L2-regularized toward zero on batches that include any human-anchored examples, keeping weak-supervision biases from contaminating the shared trunk.

To spend human labels where they matter most, TRU-RM performs uncertainty-aware, efficient elicitation. For each task instance, we sample k diverse candidates, rank them with the pointwise reward, and run a small number of right-to-left adjacent comparisons among the top-k to identify only the most informative pairs to ask humans, prioritizing adjacent pairs with high disagreement or variance from uϕ. This yields near listwise refinement at O(k) human queries per instance. Periodically, we also query “anchor” comparisons drawn from a static reference set to recalibrate and audit the model.

Finally, to prevent self-reward bias and drift, we include a debiasing monitor: a held-out suite scored by external references (task-specific metrics or curated gold comparisons). We regularize the reward model to maintain correlation with these references and limit candidate pool growth in self-improvement phases that would otherwise amplify stylistic or fluency artifacts. Together, the PID, uncertainty-weighted multi-source training, efficient elicitation, and debiasing yield a robust reward model from limited and noisy preference data.

### Rationale
TRU-RM attacks scarcity by converting abundant structure into supervision: a pseudo-demonstrator generates many high-margin comparisons, and offline outcomes supply pointwise targets. It controls noise via single-pass latent uncertainty to filter and weight pseudo-labels, plus per-source U-EMA that automatically down-weights unreliable streams. Active query selection concentrates human effort on the smallest set of pairs that maximally reduce uncertainty, using efficient adjacent comparisons to approach listwise benefits without quadratic query costs.

A shared-core, source-calibrated design lets the model learn a general reward signal while explicitly modeling biases from weak supervision, and a sampling-based structured prior improves data efficiency without heavy inference. Debiasing anchored to external references prevents the classic failure mode of self-reward pipelines that overvalue fluency or style. The result is a reward model that is better calibrated, more sample-efficient, and robust to noisy or synthetic preferences.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

8. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training.    Reinforcement learning (RL) systems typically optimize scalar reward functions that assume precise and reliable evaluation of outcomes. We introduce a dual-source uncertainty-aware reward framework that explicitly models both epistemic uncertainty in value estimation and uncertainty in human preferences. However, in practice, preference data are often noisy. Existing robust variants of DPO mainly rely on uniform objective modifications or global reweighting. While partially effective, these methods treat noisy samples as a homogeneous source of uncertainty and fail to distinguish between different noise types, leading to sub-optimal alignment robustness. In this work, we show that robust preference alignment benefits from addressing different noise types with targeted interventions rather than uniform regularization. It first uses the implicit margin from DPO log-ratio to identify heterogeneous noise patterns without relying on external reward models. For hard noise, wDPO performs a data-level intervention by sparsely correcting strongly inconsistent preference pairs. 4. Self-Exploring Language Models: Active Preference Elicitation for Online Alignment (Trans. Mach. Learn. Res. 2024, cites: 57)

Unlike offline alignment with a fixed dataset, online feedback collection from humans or AI on model generations typically leads to more capable reward models and better-aligned LLMs through an iterative process. However, achieving a globally accurate reward model requires systematic exploration to generate diverse responses that span the vast space of natural language. Random sampling from standard reward-maximizing LLMs alone is insufficient to fulfill this requirement. To address this issue, we propose a bilevel objective optimistically biased towards potentially high-reward responses to actively explore out-of-distribution regions. By solving the inner-level problem with the reparameterized reward function, the resulting algorithm, named Self-Exploring Language Models (SELM), eliminates the need for a separate RM and iteratively updates the LLM with a straightforward objective. Compared to Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), the SELM objective reduces indiscriminate favor of unseen extrapolations and enhances exploration efficiency. Our experimental results demonstrate that when fine-tuned on Zephyr-7B-SFT and Llama-3-8B-Instruct models, SELM significantly boosts the performance on instruction-following benchmarks such as MT-Bench and AlpacaEval 2.0, as well as various standard academic benchmarks in different settings. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/shenao-zhang/SELM.

5. Reward Model Routing in Alignment (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Reinforcement learning from human or AI feedback (RLHF / RLAIF) has become the standard paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs). However, most pipelines rely on a single reward model (RM), limiting alignment quality and risking overfitting. Recent work explores RM routing--dynamically selecting an RM from a candidate pool to exploit complementary strengths while maintaining $O(1)$ RM calls--but existing methods suffer from cold-start and insufficient exploration. We propose BayesianRouter, a hybrid routing framework that combines offline RM strengths learning with online Bayesian selection. In the offline stage, a multi-task router is trained on preference data to estimate per-RM reliability. In the online stage, a Bayesian Thompson sampling router performs per-query RM selection, initializing RM-specific weight vectors with offline embeddings as Gaussian priors and adaptively updating their posteriors with online rewards to adapt to the evolving policy distribution. Extensive experiments on instruction-following (AlpacaEval-2, Arena-Hard, MT-Bench) and reasoning (GSM8K, MMLU) benchmarks show that BayesianRouter consistently outperforms individual RMs, RM ensembling, and existing routing methods.

6. MAPLE: A Framework for Active Preference Learning Guided by Large Language Models (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2024, cites: 4)
   The advent of large language models (LLMs) has sparked significant interest in using natural language for preference learning. However, existing methods often suffer from high computational burdens, taxing human supervision, and lack of interpretability. To address these issues, we introduce MAPLE, a framework for large language model-guided Bayesian active preference learning. MAPLE leverages LLMs to model the distribution over preference functions, conditioning it on both natural language feedback and conventional preference learning feedback, such as pairwise trajectory rankings. MAPLE employs active learning to systematically reduce uncertainty in this distribution and incorporates a language-conditioned active query selection mechanism to identify informative and easy-to-answer queries, thus reducing the burden on humans. We evaluate MAPLE's sample efficiency and preference inference quality across two benchmarks, including a real-world vehicle route planning benchmark using OpenStreetMap data. Our results demonstrate that MAPLE accelerates the learning process and effectively improves humans' ability to answer queries.

7. Designing Reward Functions Using Active Preference Learning for Reinforcement Learning in Autonomous Driving Navigation (Applied Sciences 2024, cites: 4)
   This study presents a method based on active preference learning to overcome the challenges of designing reward functions for autonomous navigation. Results obtained from training with artificially designed reward functions may not accurately reflect human intentions. We focus on the limitations of traditional reward functions, which often fail to facilitate complex tasks in continuous state spaces. We propose the adoption of active preference learning to resolve these issues and to generate reward functions that align with human preferences. This approach leverages an individual’s subjective preferences to guide an agent’s learning process, enabling the creation of reward functions that reflect human desires. We utilize mutual information to generate informative queries and apply information gained to balance the agent’s uncertainty with the human’s response capacity, encouraging the agent to pose straightforward and informative questions. We further employ the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) method to refine the belief model, which outperforms that constructed using the Metropolis algorithm. Subsequently, we retrain the agent using reward weights derived from active preference learning. As a result, our autonomous driving vehicle can navigate between random starting and ending points without dependence on high-precision maps or routing, relying solely on its forward vision. We validate our approach’s performance within the CARLA simulation environment. Our algorithm significantly improved the success rate of autonomous driving navigation tasks that originally failed due to artificially designed rewards, increasing it to approximately 60%. Experimental results show significant improvement over the baseline algorithm, providing a solid foundation for enhancing navigation capabilities in autonomous driving systems and advancing the field of autonomous driving intelligence.

8. Dual Active Learning for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 16)
   Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences is critical to recent advances in generative artificial intelligence. Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is widely applied to achieve this objective. A key step in RLHF is to learn the reward function from human feedback. However, human feedback is costly and time-consuming, making it essential to collect high-quality conversation data for human teachers to label. Additionally, different human teachers have different levels of expertise. It is thus critical to query the most appropriate teacher for their opinions. In this paper, we use offline reinforcement learning (RL) to formulate the alignment problem. Motivated by the idea of $D$-optimal design, we first propose a dual active reward learning algorithm for the simultaneous selection of conversations and teachers. Next, we apply pessimistic RL to solve the alignment problem, based on the learned reward estimator. Theoretically, we show that the reward estimator obtained through our proposed adaptive selection strategy achieves minimal generalized variance asymptotically, and prove that the sub-optimality of our pessimistic policy scales as $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ with a given sample budget $T$. Through simulations and experiments on LLMs, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm and its superiority over state-of-the-arts.

9. ActiveUltraFeedback: Efficient Preference Data Generation using Active Learning ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has become the standard for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs), yet its efficacy is bottlenecked by the high cost of acquiring preference data, especially in low-resource and expert domains. To address this, we introduce ACTIVEULTRAFEEDBACK, a modular active learning pipeline that leverages uncertainty estimates to dynamically identify the most informative responses for annotation. Our pipeline facilitates the systematic evaluation of standard response selection methods alongside DOUBLE REVERSE THOMPSON SAMPLING (DRTS) and DELTAUCB, two novel methods prioritizing response pairs with large predicted quality gaps, leveraging recent results showing that such pairs provide good signals for fine-tuning. Our experiments demonstrate that ACTIVEULTRAFEEDBACK yields high-quality datasets that lead to significant improvements in downstream performance, notably achieving comparable or superior results with as little as one-sixth of the annotated data relative to static baselines. Our pipeline is available at https://github.com/lasgroup/ActiveUltraFeedback and our preference datasets at https://huggingface.co/ActiveUltraFeedback.

10. Reviving The Classics: Active Reward Modeling in Large Language Model Alignment (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 9)
   Building neural reward models from human preferences is a pivotal component in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and large language model alignment research. Given the scarcity and high cost of human annotation, how to select the most informative pairs to annotate is an essential yet challenging open problem. In this work, we highlight the insight that an ideal comparison dataset for reward modeling should balance exploration of the representation space and make informative comparisons between pairs with moderate reward differences. Technically, challenges arise in quantifying the two objectives and efficiently prioritizing the comparisons to be annotated. To address this, we propose the Fisher information-based selection strategies, adapt theories from the classical experimental design literature, and apply them to the final linear layer of the deep neural network-based reward modeling tasks. Empirically, our method demonstrates remarkable performance, high computational efficiency, and stability compared to other selection methods from deep learning and classical statistical literature across multiple open-source LLMs and datasets. Further ablation studies reveal that incorporating cross-prompt comparisons in active reward modeling significantly enhances labeling efficiency, shedding light on the potential for improved annotation strategies in RLHF.

Walkthrough observation and log inspection demonstrated complete coverage of six predefined objectives: every role could invoke conversational control, trace KPIs and value constraints, detect and mitigate ZIP-code bias, and reproduce full decision lineage, independent of the underlying LLM or agent library. Contributions: (1) an open-source HADA architecture, (2) a mid-range design theory for human-AI alignment in multi-agent systems, and (3) empirical evidence that framework-agnostic, protocol-compliant stakeholder agents improve accuracy, transparency, and ethical compliance in real-world decision pipelines.

In this work, we introduce novel methods to detect and evaluate prefix bias—a systematic shift in model preferences triggered by minor variations in query prefixes—in LLM-based reward models trained on such datasets. We leverage these metrics to reveal significant biases in preference models across racial and gender dimensions. Our comprehensive evaluation spans diverse open-source preference datasets and reward model architectures, demonstrating susceptibility to this kind of bias regardless of the underlying model architecture. Furthermore, we propose a data augmentation strategy to mitigate these biases, showing its effectiveness in reducing the impact of prefix bias. Our findings highlight the critical need for bias-aware dataset design and evaluation in developing fair and reliable reward models, contributing to the broader discourse on fairness in AI.

We formulate this as an optimization problem: given a preference dataset and an evaluation metric, we (1) train a performance prediction model (PPM) to predict a reward model's (RM) performance on an arbitrary combination of human and LM annotations and (2) employ a routing strategy that selects a combination that maximizes the predicted performance. We train the PPM on MultiPref, a new preference dataset with 10k instances paired with humans and LM labels. We show that the selected hybrid mixture of synthetic and direct human preferences using HyPER achieves better RM performance compared to using either one exclusively by 7-13% on RewardBench and generalizes across unseen preference datasets and other base models. We also observe the same trend in other benchmarks using Best-of-N reranking, where the hybrid mix has 2-3% better performance. In the alignment process, reward models (RMs) act as a crucial proxy for human values to guide optimization. However, it remains unclear how to evaluate and select a reliable RM for preference alignment in RALMs. To this end, we propose RAG-RewardBench, the first benchmark for evaluating RMs in RAG settings. First, we design four crucial and challenging RAG-specific scenarios to assess RMs, including multi-hop reasoning, fine-grained citation, appropriate abstain, and conflict robustness. Then, we incorporate 18 RAG subsets, six retrievers, and 24 RALMs to increase the diversity of data sources. Finally, we adopt an LLM-as-a-judge approach to improve preference annotation efficiency and effectiveness, exhibiting a strong correlation with human annotations. Based on the RAG-RewardBench, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of 45 RMs and uncover their limitations in RAG scenarios. Additionally, we also reveal that existing trained RALMs show almost no improvement in preference alignment, highlighting the need for a shift towards preference-aligned training.We release our benchmark and code publicly at https://huggingface.co/datasets/jinzhuoran/RAG-RewardBench/ for future work.

   Understanding human attitudes, preferences, and behaviors through social surveys is essential for academic research and policymaking. While recent studies explore large language models (LLMs) to simulate survey responses, most are limited to structured questions, overlook the entire survey process, and risks under-representing marginalized groups due to training data biases. We introduce AlignSurvey, the first benchmark that systematically replicates and evaluates the full social survey pipeline using LLMs. It defines four tasks aligned with key survey stages: social role modeling, semi-structured interview modeling, attitude stance modeling and survey response modeling. It also provides task-specific evaluation metrics to assess alignment fidelity, consistency, and fairness at both individual and group levels, with a focus on demographic diversity. To support AlignSurvey, we construct a multi-tiered dataset architecture: (i) the Social Foundation Corpus, a cross-national resource with 44K+ interview dialogues and 400K+ structured survey records; and (ii) a suite of Entire-Pipeline Survey Datasets, including the expert-annotated AlignSurvey-Expert (ASE) and two nationally representative surveys for cross-cultural evaluation. We release the SurveyLM family, obtained through two-stage fine-tuning of open-source LLMs, and offer reference models for evaluating domain-specific alignment. All datasets, models, and tools are available at github and huggingface to support transparent and socially responsible research.

   Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a transformative methodology in the development of conversational artificial intelligence systems. This technique bridges the gap between technical capabilities and human expectations by incorporating real-world human judgments into the training process. The implementation follows a multi-stage process including supervised fine-tuning, reward model training, and reinforcement learning optimization. While highly effective at improving model helpfulness, reducing harmful outputs, and enhancing factual consistency, RLHF implementation presents significant challenges related to data quality, scalability, reward hacking, and distribution shift. Ethical considerations surrounding bias, transparency, power dynamics, and long-term value alignment further complicate responsible deployment. Various strategies can address these challenges, including diverse annotator selection, constitutional principles, hybrid evaluation systems, and robust transparency measures. Looking forward, emerging trends such as self-supervised preference learning, multi-objective optimization, user-specific adaptation, and computational efficiency improvements will likely shape the continued evolution of this field as conversational AI becomes increasingly integrated across healthcare, customer service, education, and enterprise applications.

9. A Statistical Framework for Alignment with Biased AI Feedback (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 2)

Debiased Direct Preference Optimization (DDPO) augments standard DPO with a residual-based correction and density-ratio reweighting to mitigate systematic bias, while retaining DPO's computational efficiency. Debiased Identity Preference Optimization (DIPO) directly estimates human preference probabilities without imposing a parametric reward model. We provide theoretical guarantees for both methods: DDPO offers a practical and computationally efficient solution for large-scale alignment, whereas DIPO serves as a robust, statistically optimal alternative that attains the semiparametric efficiency bound. Empirical studies on sentiment generation, summarization, and single-turn dialogue demonstrate that the proposed methods substantially improve alignment efficiency and recover performance close to that of an oracle trained on fully human-labeled data.

Then, we surprisingly discover that dormant neurons in our critic model act as a regularization against reward overoptimization while active neurons reflect primacy bias. Motivated by these observations, we propose Temporal Diffusion Policy Optimization with critic active neuron Reset (TDPO-R), a policy gradient algorithm that exploits the temporal inductive bias of diffusion models and mitigates the primacy bias stemming from active neurons. Empirical results demonstrate the superior efficacy of our methods in mitigating reward overoptimization. Code is avaliable at https://github.com/ZiyiZhang27/tdpo.
